body:
# Example Below:
| 2006/2007 | [Apple](https://www.apple.com/), [@Apple](https://github.com/apple) | [David Maynor](https://x.com/dave_maynor) | In late 2005–2006, [Maynor](https://x.com/dave_maynor) and [Johnny Cache](https://www.darkreading.com/cyber-risk/johnny-cache-man-in-black-hat-) discovered Wi-Fi and Bluetooth driver-level attacks allowing remote control of a target machine via crafted wireless packets.

Originally slated as 0-day at Black Hat and DEF CON, the findings were disclosed to Apple first at Black Hat's suggestion. A request was made not to give the talk, but it was not a threat, and the presentation proceeded ([brief review](https://www.networkworld.com/article/842604/network-security-wireless-lessons-learned-at-a-hacker-conference.html)).

A week later, Apple began pressuring Maynor to distance MacBooks from the demo. Asked why—given a fix was already in progress—Apple's VP of Marketing implied Apple had been "very nice" about the vulnerability but could not guarantee how their user base would react.

Eight days after Black Hat, several Apple-focused blogs ran the same narrative: the [demo was fake](https://www.informationweek.com/cyber-resilience/security-firm-disclaims-mac-hack-demo). Maynor's employer was pressured to amend statements under threat of legal action. When asked about sources, the writers said only "inside Apple"—none mentioned the in-progress fix. Parts of the security community pushed back ([analysis](https://securosis.com/blog/mac-wi-fi-gruber-needs-to-let-it-go-and-maynor-and-ellch-should-ignore-the-challenge/)).

A follow-up October 2006 talk was canceled after Apple pressured Maynor's employer to prevent him from speaking ([coverage](https://www.zdnet.com/article/macbook-wifi-hacker-cancels-talk/)).

On 09/02/2006, Apple shipped three Wi-Fi driver patches addressing the issues but did not credit the researchers. Maynor had specifically targeted macOS because the Windows variants were under NDA; shortly after disclosure, the NDA was amended to cover the Apple bugs as well.

A year later, after the NDA expired, Maynor [published full technical details, including shell access](http://uninformed.org/index.cgi?v=8&a=4).

Apple fans and writers kept calling the research fake. One [offered Maynor](https://daringfireball.net/2006/09/open_challenge) a $1099 MacBook if he could hack it—apparently unaware that no-user-interaction close-access exploits trade for far more on secondary markets. The same writer later noted that Apple's VP of Marketing worked fine with [Charlie Miller](https://www.linkedin.com/in/charliemiller2/) ([source](https://daringfireball.net/2007/09/this_late_than_never)):

> Here's what Apple spokeswoman Lynn Fox — she who supposedly led an "[orchestrated attack](http://blogs.zdnet.com/Ou/?p=451)" against Maynor and Ellch — [told The Times](http://www.nytimes.com/2007/07/23/technology/23iphone.html) regarding Miller's iPhone exploit:
>
> "Apple takes security very seriously and has a great track record of addressing potential vulnerabilities before they can affect users. We're looking into the report submitted by I.S.E. and always welcome feedback on how to improve our security."
>
> No backlash. No criticism from the Mac media. No questions regarding the veracity of their claim. Why? Because he provided proof when he made the charge. That's all there is to it.

The irony: within a year, [Miller](https://www.forbes.com/sites/andygreenberg/2011/11/07/apple-exiles-a-security-researcher-from-its-developer-program-for-proof-of-concept-exploit-app/) was kicked out of Apple's Developer Program over a vulnerability disagreement. |
|
|---|---|---|---|
title: David Maynor/Johnny Cache vs Apple

body:
https://databreaches.net/2026/02/02/under-pressure-exploring-the-effect-of-legal-and-criminal-threats-on-security-researchers-and-journalists/

[..]
While threats to journalists have been documented in studies, threats to security researchers have not generated the same level. Although [attrition.org](https://attrition.org/errata/legal_threats/) and [disclose.io](https://github.com/disclose/research-threats) have compiled a wealth of significant news stories about legal threats [against security researchers](https://analyst1.com/when-the-hunter-becomes-the-hunted-the-art-and-risk-of-threat-actor-engagement/), the topic has not been systematically explored.

To address the gap in our understanding, we created a pilot survey to explore how legal threats and criminal threats affect researchers and journalists.
[..]

Overview of the sample
A total of 112 people responded to the survey.

Of these, 90 respondents (80%) self-identified as researchers and 22 respondents (20%) self-identified as journalists. When data were analyzed by researchers vs. journalists and employed vs. self-employed, we found that the largest overall subgroup (56%) identified as employed security researchers.

In response to a broad question, “Have you ever been threatened or experienced a threat in connection with your reporting or research?” **76% of researchers and 82% of journalists responded “yes,” for an overall sample statistic of 77%.**
title: Survey re: Threats - FYI / News

body:
Original disclosure removed and replaced with below. Archive.org copy was removed. Mirror of takedown version: https://archive.ph/OaSG7

https://bobdahacker.com/blog/rbi-hacked-drive-thrus/

DMCA Notice Received
We received a DMCA copyright infringement complaint from Cyble Inc., acting on behalf of Burger King, regarding our blog post about Restaurant Brands International's drive-thru system vulnerabilities.

The complaint alleges trademark infringement and claims the content "promotes illegal activity and spreads false information."

About the Complaint
Cyble Inc. is an AI-powered cybersecurity platform and DMCA takedown service startup backed by Y Combinator. Their complaint specifically states that our use of the "Burger King" trademark was unauthorized and creates "a high degree of confusion among the public that the website is in some way endorsed by/or linked with our Client."

The notice claims our security research content "promotes illegal activity and spreads false information" and that such activities are "detrimental to the goodwill and reputation" of their client and "actionable at law under gross unfair competition."

Our Response
After reviewing the complaint, we have decided to take down the blog post in question to avoid any legal complications.

While we believe our security research was conducted ethically and in the public interest, we have chosen to remove the content rather than engage in a legal dispute.
title: Bobdahacker Blog - Restaurant Brands International (RBI) / Burger King / Cyble DMCA Takedown

body:
https://floort.net/posts/belgium-unsafe-for-cvd/

This post is about the reason I will probably never try to warn any organisation in Belgium about any vulnerability again. Recently I have been dealing with an attempt at coordinated vulnerability disclosure (CVD) with an organisation in Belgium. This post is not about that, because I’m not allowed to write about it. This post explains why I believe Belgium is unsafe for people trying to do CVD. I believe it’s important to warn others so that they know what to expect and can decide for themselves.
[..]
title: Belgium is unsafe for CVD

As part of Synack's legal threat against me, they also "offered" some sort of hush money to prevent me from talking about their actions publicly. They could have prevented all that by just emailing me...but that company is toxic AF and doesn't care about researchers.

Anyway, as a consequence of this drama and to put an end to it, I will no longer be doing coordinated disclosures with them/their clients
title: Julien | MrTuxracer vs Synack

COLUMBUS, Ohio — A Franklin County judge granted a temporary restraining order against the [cybersecurity](https://www.10tv.com/article/news/local/columbus-undercover-police-information-on-dark-web/530-d806b92a-ff1d-435a-9eac-1f88076878aa) expert who revealed what kind of information and how much of it was taken during a cyberattack on the city last month.

The cybersecurity expert, Connor Goodwolf, has spoken with 10TV and other media outlets in the last few weeks detailing what information is on the dark web. Goodwolf is the name he uses for interviews and is not his legal name.

[..]
title: Connor Goodwolf vs Franklin County City

>This application was created for the Telangana State Police in 2018 and is equipped with facial recognition technology. It can compare the pictures of people with India’s national Crime and Criminal Tracking Network & Systems (CCTNS) in real-time—a nationwide database that contains millions of images of known and arrested offenders, wanted people and missing people.
title: Possible addition: Facial recognition technology is being used for multiple purposes from law enforcement. Lawsuit (India)

body:
There are two FireEye incidents already. In the HTML comments I have had this for a while and tried to get more details on Twitter:

https://twitter.com/RazorEQX/status/642124276573859841
I came under fire from [FireEye] just over a year ago for disclosing a bug they didn.t seem important to fix.
https://twitter.com/RazorEQX/status/642125189904470016
Let me talk to the attorney that defended me.  I lost a job over it.

Here's where I asked if it was one of the incidents:
https://twitter.com/securityerrata/status/643246845922250754
https://www.forbes.com/sites/thomasbrewster/2014/07/09/researcher-i-was-suspended-for-finding-flaws-in-fireeye-security-kit/?sh=6368bb336f77

Kind of surprised people still report vulns to FireEye after the other known incidents.
title: FireEye & RazorEQX

body:
As much as I appreciate you compiling and shaming companies that threaten researchers, it is clear that the research team at Secunia were mostly interested to prop their brand than actually researching and helping the open source project.

I understand that lawyering up is not the most optimal solution, but here, it is clearly a case where they are not actually pointing out a vulnerability, just trying to get a nice trophy and holding on to it as long as possible.
title: Remove VLC from the repo
